step.title: Step 35
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 85, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 59 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP moved the shape from rows 39-50 to rows 24-35 (5 UPs * 3 rows = 15 rows up). Then DOWN moved it from rows 24-35 to rows 27-38 (+3 rows).
So UP moves up by 3 rows, and DOWN moves down by 3 rows. The shape is now at rows 27-38, cols 12-26.
Looking at the image, I can see the dark gray shape has moved up. The yellow shape is still at the bottom.
I notice the dots on the black shape are now white (not sky blue). This is because I pressed SPACE earlier, which toggled the dot color.
Let me think about what the goal could be. I've tried:
1. Aligning dark gray with yellow shape - didn't work
2. Aligning dark gray with black shape's mirror position - didn't work
3. Pressing SPACE at various positions - didn't work
4. Pressing ACTION7 - toggles between states but doesn't complete the level
Maybe the goal involves the dots on the black shape. The dots change color when I press SPACE. Maybe I need to have the dots be a specific color when the dark gray shape is at a specific position.
Or maybe the goal is to move the dark gray shape to a position where it overlaps with the yellow shape in a specific way, and then press SPACE or ACTION7.
Let me look at the image more carefully. The dark gray shape is at rows 27-38, and the yellow shape is at rows 42-53. They don't overlap. The black shape on the right is at rows 39-50.
Wait, looking at the image, the yellow shape appears to be ABOVE the dark gray shape now. Let me check the segmentation.
Actually, looking at the image, the yellow shape is below the dark gray shape. The dark gray shape is at rows 27-38, and the yellow shape is at rows 42-53.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the dark gray shape to the same position as the black shape on the right panel. But the dark gray shape is on the left panel, and I don't know if it can cross the divider.
Actually, let me try to move the dark gray shape to the right panel by moving it right. At rows 27-38, let me check what RIGHT does.
Actually, I'm running out of actions and ideas. Let me try a systematic approach: move the dark gray shape to the position of the black shape (rows 39-50, cols 48-62) and see if the level completes.
But the dark gray shape is on the left panel, and the black shape is on the right panel. The divider is at cols 36-38. Can the dark gray shape cross the divider?
Let me try moving the dark gray shape right to see if it can cross the divider.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: dark gray at rows 27-38, cols 12-26
# Try moving right to see if shape can cross divider
# First move to rows 39-50 to match black shape's vertical position
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])  # 3 * 3 = 9 rows down: 27-38 -> 30-41 -> 33-44 -> 36-47
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try RIGHT to move toward divider
action(['RIGHT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray after RIGHT: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray: row 36-47, col 12-26
Dark gray after RIGHT: row 36-47, col 12-26
Level completed: False
